column3: protect and care for (someone) lovingly; hold (something) dear
column4: The old photo album was filled with cherished memories of family gatherings and childhood adventures.
column5: She kept the antique necklace in a special box, as it was a cherished heirloom passed down through generations.
column6: The annual holiday feast was a cherished tradition in their family, bringing everyone together to celebrate and reminisce.

column3: Mean or unfair artifice to perplex a cause and obscure the truth; stratagem; sharp practice; sophistry
column4: The politician's career was marred by accusations of chicanery, as opponents claimed he had manipulated election results.
column5: The company faced serious legal consequences when its chicanery in financial reporting was exposed by investigative journalists.
column6: Despite his charming demeanor, his friends were wary of his chicanery, knowing he often used clever tricks to get what he wanted.

column3: to laugh, showing pleasure and satisfaction
column4: The baby let out a delighted chortle as she watched the colorful balloons float around the room.
column5: He couldn't help but chortle at the witty jokes and clever wordplay in his favorite comic book.
column6: During their lunch break, the coworkers shared a chortle over a funny story one of them recounted about their weekend.

column3: An historical register or account of facts or events disposed in the order of time
column4: The historian's latest book is a detailed chronicle of the events leading up to the industrial revolution.
column5: She decided to chronicle her journey through Europe in a travel blog, sharing her experiences and photographs with friends and family.
column6: The documentary aimed to chronicle the rise and fall of the tech startup, providing an in-depth look at its impact on the industry.

column3: to make a loud complaint or demand
column4: The clamour of the crowd grew louder as more people joined the protest, demanding justice and reform.
column5: The teacher struggled to be heard over the clamour of excited children during recess.
column6: Walking through the bustling marketplace, she was enveloped by the clamour of vendors shouting their wares and customers haggling over prices.

column3: Disposition to forgive and spare, as offenders
column4: Despite her harsh words, he responded with clemency, choosing to forgive her and move past the argument.
column5: After the war, the king showed clemency to the rebels, offering them pardons in an effort to reunite the fractured kingdom.
column6: The governor granted clemency to the prisoner, commuting his death sentence to life in prison after new evidence suggested his possible innocence.

column3: a phrase, remark, or opinion that has very often been said or expressed before and is therefore not original and not interesting
column4: The author's latest novel was criticized for relying too heavily on clich s, such as the 'damsel in distress' and the 'hero with a dark past'.
column5: During the meeting, he tried to motivate the team, but his speech was filled with so many clich s that it felt insincere and uninspiring.
column6: The romantic comedy was entertaining, but the plot was predictable and full of clich s

column3: To persuade by gentle, insinuating courtesy, flattering, or fondling
column4: She had to coax her shy daughter to join the other children on the playground by promising they would go for ice cream afterward.
column5: The salesman tried to coax the hesitant customer into buying the new car by highlighting its impressive features and offering a generous discount.
column6: He gently coaxed the frightened kitten out from under the couch with a treat and soothing words.

column3: To restrain by force, especially by law or authority; to repress; to curb
column4: The manager was accused of trying to coerce employees into working overtime without extra pay by threatening them with job loss.
column5: The suspect claimed that his confession was not valid because the police had coerced him into admitting guilt through intimidation and prolonged questioning.
column6: She felt coerced by her friends into attending the party, even though she didn't want to go, fearing they would ostracize her if she refused.

column3: Sticking together; cleaving; as the parts of bodies; solid or fluid
column4: The manager was accused of trying to coerce employees into working overtime without extra pay by threatening them with job loss.
column5: The suspect claimed that his confession was not valid because the police had coerced him into admitting guilt through intimidation and prolonged questioning.
column6: She felt coerced by her friends into attending the party, even though she didn't want to go, fearing they would ostracize her if she refused.

column3: to work with someone else for a special purpose
column4: The two scientists decided to collaborate on the research project, combining their expertise to achieve more comprehensive results.
column5: The famous singer announced she would collaborate with a popular rapper on her next album, exciting fans of both artists.
column6: The companies agreed to collaborate on developing new technology, pooling their resources and knowledge to gain a competitive edge in the market.

column3: informal style of conversation
column4: In colloquial English, 'gonna' is often used as a contraction for 'going to'.
column5: Different regions have their own colloquial expressions and slang, making it sometimes difficult for outsiders to understand local conversations.
column6: The writer's use of colloquial language helped to create a casual and friendly tone in the dialogue of the novel.

column3: To call to remembrance by a special act or observance
column4: Every year on Veterans Day, we commemorate the bravery and sacrifice of those who served in the armed forces.
column5: A plaque was erected in the town square to commemorate the founding fathers who established the community.
column6: The couple decided to commemorate their 50th wedding anniversary with a special dinner party for family and friends.

column3: To have a beginning or origin; to start
column4: The graduation ceremony will commence at 10 a.m. in the school auditorium.
column5: The CEO will address the team before we commence our quarterly review.
column6: The trial will commence next Monday, and both the prosecution and defense are preparing their cases.

column3: To give in trust; to put into charge or keeping; to intrust; to consign; -- used with to, unto
column4: The suspect was arrested for attempting to commit burglary at the local store.
column5: She made a commitment to her friend that she would always support her through thick and thin.
column6: They decided to commit to each other and get married after years of dating.

column3: One or more persons elected or appointed, to whom any matter or business is referred
column4: The fundraising committee met to plan the upcoming charity event.
column5: The senate committee was formed to investigate the allegations of corruption within the administration.
column6: The school committee discussed the budget allocation for the next academic year.

column3: Disturbed or violent motion; agitation
column4: There was a sudden commotion outside, and when I looked, I saw a group of people gathered around a car accident.
column5: At the concert, the crowd erupted into a commotion when the famous singer appeared on stage.
column6: During the family reunion, there was a commotion in the kitchen as everyone rushed to help prepare dinner.

column3: A commercial business; the fact of being with others for togetherness or enjoyment
column4: He founded a software company that quickly became a leader in the industry.
column5: We enjoyed each other's company at the dinner party, sharing stories and laughter late into the night.
column6: The soldiers were glad for the company during their long march, finding strength and camaraderie in each other.

column3: To drive or urge with force, or irresistibly
column4: The evidence presented during the trial was enough to compel the jury to reach a guilty verdict.
column5: His strong moral values compelled him to speak out against the injustice he witnessed.
column6: The heartbreaking story compelled many to donate to the charity, moved by the plight of those in need.

column3: To put together; to construct; to build
column4: She spent weeks compiling data and conducting interviews for her research paper on climate change.
column5: He decided to compile a playlist of his favorite songs from the 80s for the road trip.
column6: The company's financial team worked diligently to compile the annual report, summarizing the year's achievements and challenges.

column3: showing smug or uncritical satisfaction with oneself or one's achievements
column4: After receiving positive feedback, she became complacent in her job, neglecting to further develop her skills.
column5: He had been complacent about his health, ignoring warning signs until he was diagnosed with a serious condition.
column6: Their marriage suffered because they became complacent, taking each other for granted and neglecting to nurture their bond.

column3: Desirous to please; courteous
column4: The complaisant host went out of his way to ensure that every guest felt comfortable and attended to, offering drinks and engaging in pleasant conversation.
column5: Despite her busy schedule, the complaisant assistant always found time to help her colleagues with their tasks, earning their gratitude and respect.
column6: Her complaisant nature made her the peacemaker of the family, often stepping in to mediate disputes and find agreeable solutions for everyone involved.

column3: the action or fact of complying with a wish or command; the state or fact of according with or meeting rules or standards
column4: The company conducted regular audits to ensure its operations were in full compliance with environmental regulations.
column5: Patient compliance with the prescribed medication regimen was crucial for the effectiveness of the treatment plan.
column6: The HR department implemented a new training program to improve employee understanding of compliance with workplace safety protocols.

column3: The state of being an accomplice; participation in guilt
column4: The investigation revealed his complicity in the fraud scheme, leading to charges being filed against him and several co-conspirators.
column5: Historians argue about the extent of the local population's complicity in the oppressive regime's actions during the occupation.
column6: She felt a pang of guilt as she realized her silence could be seen as complicity in her friend's unethical behavior at the office.

column3: having one's feelings and expression under control; calm
column4: Despite the stressful situation, she remained composed, handling the crisis with remarkable calm and efficiency.
column5: Even under intense scrutiny during the board meeting, she stayed composed and answered all questions confidently.
column6: His composed demeanor reassured everyone in the room that the situation was under control.

column3: grasp mentally; understand:
column4: The teacher used various teaching methods to ensure that all students could comprehend the complex concepts in the lesson.
column5: It took her a moment to fully comprehend the impact of the news, as the implications were far-reaching and profound.
column6: To successfully complete the project, it is essential to comprehend the intricacies of the new software system.

column3: A mate, companion, or associate
column4: The soldier risked his life to save a wounded comrade on the battlefield, demonstrating true bravery and loyalty.
column5: The politician addressed his fellow party members as comrades, emphasizing their shared goals and unity in the struggle for social justice.
column6: The revolutionaries considered each other comrades, bound together by their commitment to overthrowing the oppressive regime.

column3: To hide or withdraw from observation; to cover or keep from sight; to prevent the discovery of; to withhold knowledge of
column4: The detective suspected that the suspect was trying to conceal vital evidence that could solve the case.
column5: She wore a scarf to conceal the scar on her neck, feeling self-conscious about its appearance.
column6: The spy had to conceal his true identity while gathering intelligence behind enemy lines.

column3: excessively proud of oneself; vain
column4: His conceited attitude made it difficult for him to make friends, as he constantly boasted about his achievements and belittled others.
column5: The manager's conceited behavior alienated her team, as she rarely acknowledged their contributions and took all the credit for their success.
column6: The novel's antagonist was a conceited aristocrat who believed himself superior to everyone, leading to his eventual downfall.

column3: Capable of being conceived, imagined, or understood
column4: With the rapid advancements in technology, it is conceivable that humans might establish a colony on Mars within the next few decades.
column5: The lawyer considered every conceivable defense strategy to ensure the best possible outcome for her client.
column6: It's conceivable that the traffic delay was caused by the construction work on the main highway.

column3: jointly arranged or carried out; coordinated:
column4: The team made a concerted effort to meet the deadline, working overtime and coordinating closely to complete the project on time.
column5: The activists organized a concerted campaign to raise awareness about environmental issues, holding protests and lobbying policymakers.
column6: The community made a concerted effort to support the local food bank, donating goods and volunteering their time to help those in need.

column3: intended or likely to placate or pacify:
column4: The ambassador's conciliatory remarks helped defuse tensions between the two countries, paving the way for productive negotiations.
column5: She made a conciliatory gesture by offering a sincere apology, hoping to mend their strained relationship.
column6: The CEO's conciliatory approach in the meeting reassured investors and stakeholders, leading to a successful resolution of the dispute.

column3: Expressing much in a few words; condensed
column4: Her essay was praised for its concise arguments and clear structure, making it easy for readers to understand.
column5: During the presentation, he delivered concise explanations that captured the key points without unnecessary details.
column6: The recipe provided concise steps, making it simple for even beginners to follow and prepare the dish.

column3: be of the same opinion; agree
column4: After reviewing the evidence, the jury unanimously concurred on the defendant's guilt.
column5: The board members concur that expanding into new markets is the best strategy for company growth.
column6: Several studies concur that regular exercise contributes significantly to overall health and well-being.

column3: express complete disapproval of; sentence (someone) to a particular punishment, especially death:
column4: The court condemned the defendant to life imprisonment for the heinous crime of murder.
column5: The community leaders strongly condemn any form of discrimination based on race, religion, or gender.
column6: The government issued a statement condemning the recent terrorist attacks and vowing to take decisive action against the perpetrators.

column3: an attitude that someone or something is unworthy of one's consideration or respect
column4: Her tone was filled with condescension as she explained the basic concepts to the new employee, implying superiority.
column5: He couldn't help but feel a sense of condescension from his wealthy relatives, who often made remarks about his modest lifestyle.
column6: The manager's habit of speaking down to her team members with condescension created a tense and demoralizing work atmosphere.

column3: To pardon; to forgive
column4: The company policy clearly states that we do not condone any form of harassment or discrimination in the workplace.
column5: As a parent, I cannot condone my child's behavior of cheating on exams; instead, I must teach them the importance of honesty and integrity.
column6: Despite his popularity, many people cannot condone his reckless behavior and lavish lifestyle, seeing it as irresponsible.

column3: making a certain situation or outcome likely or possible; helpful
column4: A quiet and organized study environment is conducive to concentration and effective learning.
column5: Regular breaks and a supportive team atmosphere are conducive to higher productivity in the workplace.
column6: The stimulating and relaxed atmosphere of the artist's studio was conducive to producing innovative and inspiring artwork.

column3: grant (a title, degree, benefit, or right); have discussions; exchange opinions
column4: The committee will confer next week to finalize the budget for the upcoming fiscal year.
column5: The university conferred an honorary degree upon the renowned scientist for her contributions to the field of medicine.
column6: Before making a decision, it's important to confer with legal counsel to ensure compliance with regulations.

column3: To restrain within limits; to restrict
column4: The zookeepers confine the lions to a large enclosure where visitors can safely observe them.
column5: His fear of flying confines him to traveling by train or car for long distances.
column6: The river acts as a natural barrier, confining the city to a narrow strip of land along its banks.

column3: take or seize (someone's property) with authority
column4: The police confiscated a large quantity of illegal drugs during the raid.
column5: The teacher confiscated the students' cell phones because they were using them during class.
column6: Customs officers confiscated prohibited items from travelers entering the country.

column3: To mingle and blend, so that different elements can not be distinguished; to confuse
column4: The riddle confounded everyone at the party, as no one could figure out the answer.
column5: The sudden change in weather confounded the meteorologists, who had predicted a clear day.
column6: His unexpected kindness confounded her, leaving her speechless and touched by his generosity.

column3: (of a person) pleasing or liked on account of having qualities or interests that are similar to one's own; (of a thing) pleasant or agreeable because suited to one's taste or inclination
column4: She found the party atmosphere congenial, with friendly conversations and welcoming hosts.
column5: The team members enjoyed a congenial relationship, which made collaboration on projects smooth and enjoyable.
column6: He preferred living in a congenial climate, with mild winters and plenty of sunshine throughout the year.

column3: an expert judge in matters of taste:
column4: He is known as a connoisseur of fine art, with a deep knowledge of various painting styles and artists.
column5: As a connoisseur of wine, she could distinguish the subtle flavors and aromas of different varieties.
column6: The music connoisseur could identify the composer of each piece within seconds of hearing the opening notes.

column3: a person's moral sense of right and wrong, viewed as acting as a guide to one's behaviour
column4: His conscience wouldn't allow him to keep the money he found on the street, so he turned it in to the police.
column5: She followed her conscience and refused to participate in the unethical business practices suggested by her colleagues.
column6: His conscience troubled him after realizing he had hurt his friend's feelings with his thoughtless remarks.

column3: wishing to do one's work or duty well and thoroughly
column4: She is a conscientious employee who always completes her tasks with thoroughness and attention to detail.
column5: As a conscientious citizen, he recycles regularly and volunteers in community clean-up efforts.
column6: His conscientious study habits paid off when he earned top grades in his exams.

column3: aware of and responding to one's surroundings; having knowledge of something
column4: She was conscious of the noise outside and closed the window to block it out.
column5: Even in her sleep, she was conscious of the fact that she needed to wake up early the next morning.
column6: He made a conscious decision to donate a portion of his salary to charity each month.

column3: a general agreement:
column4: After a lengthy discussion, the committee reached a consensus on the budget allocation for the next fiscal year.
column5: The leaders of the political parties gathered to seek a consensus on the new legislation before presenting it to parliament.
column6: There is a growing consensus among scientists that climate change is primarily caused by human activities.

column3: To agree in opinion or sentiment; to be of the same mind; to accord; to concur
column4: Before conducting the medical procedure, the doctor obtained informed consent from the patient.
column5: She gave her consent to the proposal, agreeing to marry him.
column6: Minors need parental consent to participate in certain activities, such as signing contracts or undergoing medical treatment.

column3: To keep in a safe or sound state; to save; to preserve; to protect
column4: The organization works tirelessly to conserve wildlife habitats and protect endangered species.
column5: Switching off lights when leaving a room is a simple way to conserve energy and reduce electricity bills.
column6: They canned fruits and vegetables from their garden to conserve them for the winter months.
